title: SFC QuestionBank
subtitle: Scrum Fundamentals Certified
description: Imported Scrum Fundamentals Certified question bank items from the provided PDF source.

questions.q: Which process best supports the progressive improvement of requirements during a Scrum project?
questions.explain: Backlog grooming or refinement is the Scrum activity used to clarify, revise, split, and reprioritize requirements as the team learns more.
questions.deep: Progressive elaboration of requirements happens through repeated backlog refinement, not through shipping or a status event. Retrospectives improve the process, while refinement improves the work itself.

questions.q: During which Scrum event does the Scrum Team discuss day-to-day progress updates?
questions.explain: The Daily Standup exists specifically for quick progress updates, coordination, and identification of blockers.
questions.deep: Daily coordination is time-boxed into the standup so the team can inspect progress and adapt its plan for the next 24 hours.

questions.q: Which role is identified in the Create Project Vision process?
questions.explain: The Product Owner is the role most directly responsible for articulating and owning the product vision.
questions.deep: Scrum depends on clear product direction. The Product Owner connects business goals, stakeholder expectations, and backlog priorities through that vision.

questions.q: Which role is primarily responsible for the Business Justification aspect in a Scrum project?
questions.explain: The Product Owner is accountable for maximizing value and maintaining the business case behind the product work.
questions.deep: Business justification belongs with the role that prioritizes value and represents stakeholder needs, which is why it sits with the Product Owner rather than the delivery team.

questions.q: Which Scrum principle best helps teams manage change while building products that satisfy customer needs?
questions.explain: Iterative development allows teams to inspect outcomes frequently and adjust the product as requirements or feedback evolve.
questions.deep: Short iterations reduce the cost of being wrong. Instead of locking in one large plan, the team learns and adapts as the product takes shape.

questions.q: Which role group includes customers, users, and sponsors who frequently interact with the Scrum Core Team?
questions.explain: Stakeholders are the broader group with an interest in the product outcome, including customers, users, and sponsors.
questions.deep: Scrum depends on regular stakeholder feedback so the team can validate whether it is building the right thing, not just building it correctly.

questions.q: Which statement best describes the Scrum principle of Collaboration?
opts: This principle treats time as the main planning constraint and manages work through fixed time-boxes
This principle emphasizes self-management by letting workers organize themselves instead of relying on command-and-control structures
This principle focuses on awareness, articulation, and appropriation, and treats project work as a shared value-creation process
This principle emphasizes maximizing business value by ordering work so the highest-value items are delivered first
questions.explain: Collaboration in Scrum is about people working together with shared awareness, coordinated action, and adaptation of tools and practices to create value.
questions.deep: Scrum is not just a flow of tasks. It depends on continuous interaction among team members and stakeholders so decisions, feedback, and delivery remain aligned.

questions.q: Which of the following pairs is incorrectly matched?
opts: Prioritized delivery: Focus only on business value
Appropriation: Adapting technology to one's own situation
Adaptation: Risk identification is performed and iterated throughout the project
Collaboration: Stakeholders and development team interact continually
questions.explain: Prioritized delivery is about maximizing business value, but not about focusing only on business value while ignoring other constraints such as risk, feasibility, and dependencies.
questions.deep: Good Scrum prioritization balances value with timing, effort, uncertainty, and learning. Reducing it to one dimension oversimplifies the decision process.

questions.q: What artifact is often used to track the status of work and activities being carried out by the Scrum Team?
questions.explain: A Scrumboard provides visible, current status of tasks and work in progress for the team.
questions.deep: Its value is transparency. By making work visible, the team can coordinate more easily and spot blocked or unfinished work quickly.

questions.q: Which set of Scrum practices most directly supports transparency?
opts: Scrumboard, Impediment Log, and Daily Standup
Scrumboard, Daily Standup, and Video Conferencing
Scrumboard, Impediment Log, Daily Standup, and Video Conferencing
questions.explain: Transparency in Scrum is strengthened by visible work tracking, visible impediments, and regular progress communication.
questions.deep: Video conferencing can support communication, but it is not itself a core Scrum transparency mechanism. The key idea is making work, blockers, and status easy to inspect.
